creator: alley1@llnl.gov
text:
> > At Thu, 27 Sep 2001 16:31:50 -0700 (PDT),
 > > Ed Alley wrote:
 
 > > Incidentally: ...
 > > the FreeBSD users will be left behind unless the version
 > > number in the Makefile is changed from 1.5.02 to 1.5.
 
 At Fri, 28 Sep 2001 00:20:15 (GMT),
 FUJISHIMA Satsuki wrote:
 
 > FreeBSD ports system does not like to track automatically "the latest
 > and greatest" software released without changing its filename. ...
 
 "the latest and greatest" is not the correct term here. The term should
 be "bug fixes". To me, the term "latest and greatest" implies changes
 or enhancements in the distribution beyound what is offered. The
 meaning of the teeny version number for the Yorick distribution
 is "bug fixes" not "latest and greatest". This I have on the
 authority of the author of Yorick, Dave Munro, who is a colleague
 of mine here at the laboratory. I submitted this port for him
 since he is not familiar with FreeBSD. (He is a Linux guy).
 
 > It breaks the meaning of distinfo and we don't want to fix it
 > everytime. 
 
 I think what you are telling me is that you don't trust the
 originator of Yorick to stick to the "bug fix" meaning of
 the teeny version number (in this case 02). So that he
 could break the port by an enhancement and you lose control
 over the stability of the compilation of the port.
 
 What worries me is that the originator eventually intends to
 delete the earlier teeny version files in the archive in order
 to conserve space as bugs are corrected. So he will eventually
 break the port anyway when yorick-1.5.02.tgz is deleted!
 
 > If you want ports-current handling latest software, please
 > send-pr to report it. Thanks.
 
 I can do this with a send-pr each and every time a bug-fix
 is placed in the archive, but it will involve a patch that
 will consist of just a change to the version number like:
 
 	PORTVERSION= 1.5.02 ---> PORTVERSION= 1.5.03
 
 This has to go through the ports process just like any other
 port including the time lag between submission and execution.
 Plus, it clutters up the ports submittals and takes up your
 time changing a teeny version number in a port when you could
 be doing more important things. Ironically, you end up doing
 exactly what you didn't want to do:
 
 >	"... we don't want to fix it everytime."
 
 P.S.
 
 Where can I find a definition of the meaning of "distinfo"?
 
 P.P.S.
 
 I'm not trying to be a malcontent here. I just think that your
 position on this is going to cause the ports maintainers more
 work than is necessary. Yes, it means that the originator could
 break the port; but, isn't it then incumbent upon me (or someone
 like me) to fix it with a send-pr? Or is it your position that
 the archive should always be static? otherwise, don't submit
 a port. If the answer to this is yes, then perhaps we should
 withdraw the Yorick port. This would perhaps be a disappointment
 to some people, including Dave and me. (:-<)
 
 --------
 
 		Ed Alley
 		wea@llnl.gov
